What do you think?

author: @svenhansen7352
text:
The ROI depends on the type of intent to build a wind turbine. If you just want to make money with it (subsidies and energy market prices) then it boils down to big HAWT's (3MW +).
Vawt's cannot yet be produced at this level of performance.
However, if you want to be self-sufficient and meet your own needs, in my opinion you are better off with a VAWT, because you can also operate it discreetly in urban areas. Their performance can also be increased by front-mounted air deflectors, which also cover the rotors so that there is no visual disturbance. VAWT'S are also considered bird-safe in contrast to the large HAWT's

author: @adymode
text: The available energy in wind is not proportional to the average windspeed - it is proportional to the Mean Cube of the windspeed. This can have a very different value from the average eg. average speed of 1 if constant the Mean Cube will be 1. If half the time it blows at 2 and half at 0 the Mean Cube will be 4.

author: @Rcbeacon
text:
I experimented with a few small HAWT's back in the mid 1980s to power remote radio repeaters.  On a pole on a hill top a small generator (300W or so) did a great job, plenty of power.  Large solar panels were new at the time, very expensive and didn't work very well.
As you suggest, it's more complex than it first appears.  Is the claim of 1000W dishonest because you only get 50W in a 8kt wind - not really.  Is a 400Kg trailer rating deceptive if you only carry 50Kg in it.  They may be stretching the truth a bit because on a hill top in a gale it would probably fly apart not long after achieving 1000W output.  It would be nice if they said, typical output 100W, peak output 1000W - but engineering would have to sneak that past the marketing department.
I thought about putting up a small HAWT here at home.  I bought a unit rated at 400W max (in a strong wind) but didn't bother putting it up after finding that our average wind speed is only 10Kph and most of the time it would do nothing.

author: @chrisgriffiths2533
text:
Gusts Robert, Average Wind Speed does not take Peak Winds into Consideration.
Hence the Ratings of 1Kilowatt may be the Rating of the Internal Alternator or Generator.
In turn Perhaps at Max Speed your Wind Generator will Produce 1 Kilowatt but it has been designed to ensure it can not go past max speed because of Failure.   Hence the Problem is the Design and how to Better Manage and Maximise Occasional Gusts, High Winds?.
Another Good Topic.

The same applies even to so-called "green" industries - instead of getting back to basics, for them it also does seem to be about the bike!

author: @tg_privat
text: A silboat sails much slower if it sails away from wind (with the wind) than sailing perpendicular to the wind. This is why the horizontal axis turbines are more efficient. If there is only enough windspeed and no turbulece. At small windspeeds and in turbulent condititons the vertical axis wins. Square sail vs. triangular sail is same difference. Square one uses only drag, the other is a wing, where the lift force is resulted by air pressure difference between the top and bottom surfaces of the wing. Preasure difference is caused by different top/bottom air speeds caused by shape of the wing.

author: @dexterdixon2000
text: 4.40 - that's not the maximum it can generate. It's just the maximum it can generate in the average wind. On a stronger windy day (like today in Derbyshire) it would make a lot more than 50W. I don't like the Tesup and I agree it's not on the call it a 1kW machine. But it's also not on to say the maximum it can make is at the average windspeed. That average is made up of 10m/s days and 0m/s days.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Didn’t mention ‘Amortization’.  £30 spent on a turbine which will keep on working for 25 yrs is a lot cheaper than one that costs £10 but lasts only 25 months. Ergs ! Only Rob's rowing cart grind them out now since the introduction of SI units.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I was thinking along the lines of: 
When £10  turbine (or any other mechanical contrivance) has been built and proven — attend  to the details. These would include protecting it against corrosion. Ensuring the parts are sturdy enough not to brake from fatigue. Endeavour to make future servicing and maintenance easier,  e.g., any nuts, screws, terminals etc., that may need to be undone are coated with silicone grease. Coils protected with resin and so on.  A godsend (repeat this out loud, so as to remember to do it) is to included a small weather proof container,  into which to place useful notes. E.g. Type and part number of carbon brushes (perhaps include a spare) and anything else about the apparatus that you will (mark my words) defiantly forget over time. Saves hours of having to suss things out all over again (you’ll appreciate this better if standing at the top of a ladder on a cold winters day). The container can be very small, such as a pill bottle secured by a U shaped pipe clamp. Hey Presto, £10 turbine is now a less expensive £30 machine. It used to be the norm to build things to have a service life of 25 years (even motor cars).  Hard evidence of this fact can be seen in awesome motion, upon a visit to any Living Heritage Museum. And easily achievable today.

author: @contrarianviewpoint
text:
Wind turbines are not economically viable.   If they were they wouldn't need government subsidizes. 

When the real engineers get involved and do true cost analysis they all come back with that conclusion but nobody listens.   The trouble is that many don't carry out the true costs.   Efficiency is one of the first things that gets overlooked but more than anything many don't look at the cost of construction, transmission, maintenance, liability and lifespan. 

In the US we already have 10 year old wind turbines basically falling apart.  In 10 more years they are gonna be junk. 

People were thinking they could just plant all these windmills all over the country and they would just sit there and make electricity forever and for free. Nope.  Doesn't work that way.  Just because the wind is free doesn't make the electricity free.  Indeed it can cost more than other generation methods. 

In the US natural gas has become the common way to create electricity.  It features a retail price of about 0.12 cents per killowatt.  It's a hard price to beat.   If you can get it, buy it.   Solar, wind or whatever it costs more.  Nuclear might be cheaper but not when you calculate it down to its true costs. 

Thumbs up on the video.   I've tried numerous times to explain to people the efficiency trap you get into with solar and windmills.   Most people don't listen.   They just think free sun and free wind.

replies.author: @dexterdixon2000
replies.text: Big wind is already cheaper than coal and gas. As is large scale solar. Levelised cost of energy calculations take into account maintainace and decommissioning.

replies.author: @huckmiddeke665
replies.text:
Problem with natural gas is that its not renewable and that burning it has other byproducts.
Also the cost of the natural gas shouldn't be measured in money, as always when taking something from the earth the price tag is not a financial one. Windpower based on recycled materials is impossible to beat (and not even comparable) with natural gas.
